title: ‘A pope who uses his brain’: Vatican locals and visitors take sides in Leo v Trump spat - The Guardian
content: While some US visitors back their president, shopkeepers who serve the papacy and tourists support the pontiff On the wall of the back room of an optician’s in Borgo Pio, a neighbourhood in Rome that borders the Vatican, hang the photos of five popes dating back to the late 1970s, charting both the recent history of Catholic church leaders and the shop itself. As its owner, Walter Colantini, who fitted glasses for one of the pontiffs, gestured towards them, he recalled the diplomatic strain between the Vatican and White House over the 1991 Gulf war. But, he said, nothing compared to the rupture provoked by Donald Trump in response to Pope Leo’s criticisms of the US-Israeli strikes on Iran. In a tirade on social media, Trump said Leo, the first ever US pontiff, was “weak on crime” and “terrible on foreign policy” and was only elected pope because Trump himself was in the White House. Trump then shared an AI-generated image of himself depicted as Christ-like before deleting it. “Trump is off the wall, he has no limits,” said Colantini. It is a sentiment shared by fellow shopkeepers in Borgo Pio, who have a long history of providing services to the Vatican, as they struggle to absorb the deterioration in its relations with Washington. Trump’s outburst was followed by the US vice-president, JD Vance, a Catholic, telling Fox News the Vatican should “stick to morality” and church affairs and let the president “stick to dictating US public policy”. Raniero Mancinelli, a tailor who produces ecclesiastical robes and jewellery, said: “The issue with Trump is that we don’t know what he is thinking or doing from one day to the next. He is saying and doing very strange things. When he was elected, he spoke about peace and ending wars, instead the world is in this very worrying situation.” People visiting St Peter’s Square on Tuesday were also troubled by the clash and expressed solidarity with Leo. “Trump has crossed every moral border, but I am no longer surprised,” said Andries DeWinter, a choir director from Belgium. “He insisted on making America great again, but instead he’s sinking it. Pope Leo, on the other hand, speaks with truth, value and meaning – the total opposite to Trump.” Rhoda, a visitor from the UK, said: “Trump is just out for himself. I think Leo is great and I just hope he keeps standing up to him.” However, two US Catholics who the Guardian spoke to said that while they respected the pope and the church, they supported Trump. “We want peace but we believe he’s making the world safe,” said Susan. “He is a little arrogant, but that’s just the way he is.” Leo, considered more mild-mannered and tactful than his often hasty and divisive predecessor, Francis, was elected pope in May last year and since then has moved from careful stewardship, such as urging ceasefires and diplomacy to end wars, to striking a bolder moral tone. He has increasingly condemned the US-Israeli war in Iran and wider conflict in the Middle East, especially after Trump’s threat last week to wipe out “a whole civilisation” in Iran. The church’s cardinals have also made harsh criticisms. In an interview with CBS on Monday night, Trump confirmed he lashed out at Leo after watching an interview with three influential US cardinals on the networks’s 60 Minutes who called out Trump’s policies and backed up Leo’s remarks. “He’s wrong on the issues,” Trump said. “I don’t think he should be getting into politics. I think he probably learned that from this.” But Leo is not expected to back down. In response to Trump’s verbal attack, he said he did not fear the US administration and would continue to speak out against war. The clash generated widespread solidarity towards the pontiff from European politicians across the spectrum. Italy’s prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, one of Trump’s main allies in Europe, said the president’s remarks towards Leo were “unacceptable”. In response, in an interview with the Italian daily Corriere della Sera on Tuesday, Trump said Meloni was the one who was “unacceptable” due to her unwillingness to join the Iran war. “I’m shocked at her. I thought she had courage, but I was wrong,” he said. It has taken some Italian Catholics time to get used to Leo, especially after the era of Francis, who was admired for his humble yet charismatic demeanour. “But Leo has been a good pope so far, very calm,” said Mancinelli. Colantani said Leo differed in style from his predecessor, but was possibly more effective. “Leo was chosen for the role because he’s the right person to lead the church during this period,” he said. “He needs to act in order to find peace in a world where there is war. I really like him; from what he says in his declarations and homilies, he’s a pope who uses his brain.”

title: Obituary for Patricia (McLemore) Usher - Ronald V. Hall Funeral Home
content:
We would like to offer our sincere support to anyone coping with grief. Enter
                    your email below for our complimentary daily grief messages. Messages run for up to one year and you
                    can stop at any time. Your email will not be used for any other purpose. Mrs. Patricia McLemore Usher, age 90, of the New Branch Community, died Tuesday, April 14, 2026, at the Community Hospice House in Vidalia after a brief illness. She was a native of Toombs County, growing up in Vidalia, and was a 1954 graduate of Vidalia High School. She married her husband, and they moved to New Branch in 1955. She was a homemaker and a member of Vidalia Church of God where she sang in the choir and served as church secretary. She enjoyed cooking and feeding whoever walked in her home, camping in the Smoky Mountains and at Hilton Head Island. She is preceded in death by her husband, Ben Hill Usher; son-in-law, Ronnie Youmans; parents, William “Bill” McLemore and Ella Williamson McLemore; one sister, Betty Kate McLemore Todd; and two brothers, Billy McLemore and John “Pee Wee” McLemore. Mrs. Usher is survived by three daughters, Ann Usher Lewis and husband Donnie, Debbie Usher Youmans, and Janice Usher Sammons and husband Ricky, all of New Branch; five grandchildren, Lori Lewis and Greg Horton of Vidalia, Jennifer Corbitt and husband Andy of Nichols, Lacey Thigpen and husband Alan of the Ohoopee Community, Amanda Edwards and husband Jeffery of New Branch, and Ben Sammons and Taylor Colson of Statesboro; seventeen great-grandchildren, six great-great-grandchildren, one sister, Elouise Seidel; two brothers, Gene McLemore and wife Sue Ellen, and Mercer McLemore and wife Phanetta; one brother-in-law, Toby Todd; one sister-in-law, Elizabeth McLemore, all of Vidalia; and numerous nieces and nephews. The funeral service will be held on Friday afternoon, April 17th, 2026, at 3:00 at Vidalia Church of God with Pastor Josh May officiating. Interment will follow at Pine Crest Cemetery. The family will receive friends at the church on Friday afternoon from 1:00 until just prior to the service. Pallbearers are Ben Sammons, Austin Lewis, A.J. Corbitt, Trent Corbitt, Tyler Best, Connor Thigpen, Adam Edwards, Jacob Edwards, Eli Edwards, and Koda King. In lieu of flowers, memorial donations may be made to the Vidalia Church of God, 401 Adams St., Vidalia, GA 30474. Ronald V. Hall Funeral Home of Vidalia is in charge of arrangements. To send flowers
                            to the family or plant a tree
                            in memory of Patricia (McLemore)  Usher, please visit our floral store.

title: Latest Federal Court Cases: ironSource Ltd. v. Digital Turbine, Inc. - April 2026 - JD Supra
content:
ironSource Ltd. v. Digital Turbine, Inc., Appeal No. 2024-1831 (Fed. Cir. April 7, 2026) In its only precedential patent opinion last week, the Federal Circuit dismissed an appeal from a post-grant review in which substitute claims were permitted, finding that patent challenger ironSource failed to establish Article III standing for its appeal.  The Court’s ruling turned on the fact that all originally-challenged claims of the patent at issue had been found unpatentable, and that ironSource had not shown how its planned product features were implicated by the amended—as opposed to original—claims. In the proceedings below, ironSource had challenged claims of Digital Turbine’s U.S. Patent No. 11,157,256, which is directed to background downloading of mobile device applications.  The Patent Trial and Appeal Board found all challenged claims unpatentable in light of a prior decision involving the ’256 patent’s parent, but granted Digital Turbine’s contingent motion to amend with narrowed substitute claims, over ironSource’s opposition.  In appealing that decision, ironSource argued that Digital Turbine had made “veiled threats” of enforcing its patents against “Click to Install” (C2I) features of ironSource’s software product; that ironSource had ultimately removed those features as a result; and that it was prevented from re-introducing the features by the Board’s finding on the substitute claims. While Article III standing is not required to bring a PGR challenge before the Patent Office, it is required to appeal an adverse ruling in the judiciary.  On review, the Federal Circuit panel relied on precedent holding that where an appellant challenging patent validity relies on potential infringement liability as a basis for standing, “it must establish that it has concrete plans for future activity that creates a substantial risk of future infringement or likely cause the patentee [sic] to assert a claim of infringement.”  Here, the Court found ironSource failed “to demonstrate concrete plans to reintroduce this product with features that are implicated by the substitute claims at issue.”  (Original emphasis.) ironSource’s evidence focused on the original claims of the ’256 patent and its parent patent—which had been in force when Digital Turbine’s alleged infringement threats had occurred—but it was undisputed that the substitute claims surviving the PGR had added material narrowing limitations.  Because ironSource’s evidence was “silent as to these limitations and their relationship to the C2I features ironSource seeks to add to its products,” the panel found that ironSource had not established an actual or imminent injury-in-fact sufficient to confer standing, and dismissed the appeal. title: RCI and RWJBH Physician-Scientist Receives V Foundation for Cancer Research Award to Advance Studies - RWJBarnabas Health
content:
Important Update: Masking and visitation guidelines have been updated. New Brunswick, NJ, April 14, 2026– Christian Hinrichs, MD, Co-Director of the Duncan and Nancy MacMillan Cancer Immunology and Metabolism Center of Excellence and Chief of the Section of Cancer Immunotherapy, RWJBarnabas Health, has been awarded a four-year, $800,000 translational research grant from the V Foundation for Cancer Research to develop next-generation immunotherapies for cancers caused by Human Papillomavirus (HPV). HPV is a leading cause of several cancer types, including cervical, throat, and anal cancers. Dr. Hinrichs’s research group has pioneered T cell therapies that target HPV in these cancers. However, current T cell therapies require custom manufacturing for each individual patient, limiting their accessibility. The V Foundation-funded project aims to overcome this barrier by developing treatments that can be produced at scale. Dr. Hinrichs recently disclosed initial findings from a Phase II clinical trial of a genetically engineered T cell therapy targeting HPV, where six out of 10 patients achieved substantial tumor shrinkage, including two complete tumor regressions. Building on this clinical progress, the V Foundation-funded project will develop T cell receptor-based T cell engagers (TCR-TCEs), a new class of immunotherapy. TCR-TCEs are fusion proteins that redirect a patient’s own immune cells to attack cancer. Unlike personalized cell therapies, TCR-TCEs can be manufactured in large batches, stored, and administered to patients when needed. “The goal of this research is to develop a broadly accessible treatment for cancers caused by HPV,” said Dr. Christian Hinrichs. “By developing ‘off-the-shelf’ immunotherapies that are more available and cost effective, we hope to deliver our T cell-based treatments to more patients.” About Rutgers Cancer Institute As New Jersey’s only National Cancer Institute-designated Comprehensive Cancer Center, Rutgers Cancer Institute, together with RWJBarnabas Health, provides patients access to the most advanced treatment options including clinical trials close to home at our facilities throughout the state. Our groundbreaking cancer research is the engine that drives access to the most advanced cancer treatments, where our discoveries become your care. This has been enhanced with the opening of the state’s first and only freestanding cancer hospital, the Jack and Sheryl Morris Cancer Center. Visit: cinj.org. About RWJBarnabas HealthRWJBarnabas Health is New Jersey’s largest and most comprehensive academic health system, caring for more than five million people annually. Nationally renowned for quality and safety, the system includes 14 hospitals and 9,000 affiliated physicians integrated to provide care at more than 700 patient care locations. RWJBarnabas Health’s commitment to enhancing access to care includes a transformative partnership with Rutgers University, including the Rutgers Cancer Institute — the state's only NCI-designated Comprehensive Cancer Center, and Rutgers Robert Wood Johnson Medical School. RWJBarnabas Health partners with its communities to build and sustain a healthier New Jersey. It provides patient-centered care in a compassionate manner and is the state’s largest safety-net provider and leader in addressing the social determinants of health. RWJBarnabas Health provides food to the hungry, housing for the homeless and economic opportunities to those most vulnerable. About the V Foundation for Cancer ResearchThe V Foundation for Cancer Research was founded in 1993 by ESPN and the late Jim Valvano, legendary North Carolina State University basketball coach, ESPN commentator and member of the Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame. The V Foundation has funded over $458 million in game-changing cancer research grants in North America through a competitive process strictly supervised by a world-class Scientific Advisory Committee. Because the V Foundation has an endowment to cover administrative expenses, 100% of direct donations is awarded to cancer research and programs. The V team is committed to funding the best scientists to accelerate Victory Over Cancer® and save lives. To learn more, visit v.org. To speak to someone about choosing a doctor,
				click here.

title: Ian V. O'Dierno Obituary April 14, 2026 - Newcomer Green Bay
content:
Please select what you would like included for printing: Copy the text below and then paste that into your favorite email application Ian V. O' Dierno, 34, of Abrams, passed away peacefully surrounded by his loved ones on April 14, 2026. He was born on December 19, 1991, to Ken and Margaret (Reinhard) O'Dierno in Escanaba, Michigan. He had a typical boys childhood with his older brother, Ryan. He graduated from Oconto Falls High School in 2009 and attended Michigan Tech. He worked many years at Sandalwood Golf Club and worked at Kia Metro in Madison and Ford Dealership in Champaign, Illinois. He most recently was employed with Heights Finance in Green Bay. He met the love of his life, Marissa Salinas, in June 2021, where Ian drove two hours to her so they can go on their first date. Together they lived in Champaign with their dog, Geely, before moving to Green Bay to be closer to his family and friends. Ian and Marissa loved going to the zoo, exploring different parts of Wisconsin, trying new food together and genuinely loved enjoying each others company. As Ian said and was a huge believer in, "It was not what you were doing, but who you were with that mattered." Ian and Marissa were to be married in October of this year, which he was very excited for and looking forward to his future with Marissa and her family. Ian loved golfing, cooking, cars, Star Trek and spending time outdoors exploring. He found joy in the little challenges in life to prove he was right. Ian was typically an introverted person, but was his genuine self around his family and friends. He was also known for his comebacks, people who met him usually thought of him as sassy and funny. Ian is survived by his loving fiancée, Marissa; his parents, Ken and Margaret; his brother, Ryan and his wife, Nikole; his niece and God-daughter, Kadence; Marissa's parents, Sylvia and Gregorio Salinas Sr.; brother-in-law to be, Gregorio Salinas Jr; his Uncle Joe Reinhard; and many other aunts, uncles, cousins and family and friends. He was preceded in death by his both maternal grandparents, Vincent and Babe Reinhard; and paternal grandparents, Eugene and Margaret O'Dierno. A Viewing for Ian will be held on Friday, April 17, 2026 from 4-7 PM at Newcomer Cremations, Funerals, Receptions, 340 S. Monroe Ave., Green Bay, Wisconsin. A Funeral Service will begin at 7 PM until the time of the service at the funeral home. There will be a time during the service to share memories of Ian, all are encourage to share. In lieu of flowers, donations can be made in Ian's honor to the Green Bay Animal Humane Society, as animals shared a huge part of his heart. title: Commonwealth v. Darrell J. Lee - City of Norfolk (.gov)
content: NORFOLK, Va. — Darrell Jamar Lee, 37, was sentenced on April 3 to serve six years in prison after police found Mr. Lee, a previously convicted felon, drunk and asleep at the wheel last year with a firearm on his person and distribution-level quantities of narcotics in his vehicle. On July 30, 2025, Norfolk Police officers saw Mr. Lee asleep at the wheel of an operating vehicle at the intersection of East Brambleton and Park avenues while the traffic light was green. The officers walked up to Mr. Lee’s vehicle, saw him slumped over the steering wheel, and tapped on the driver’s window to wake Mr. Lee. When Mr. Lee complied with officers and got out of the vehicle, they noticed that Mr. Lee was not steady on his feet, was slurring his words, and had glassy eyes. Mr. Lee told the officers that he had consumed alcohol earlier that night at a lounge, agreed to participate in field sobriety tests (during each of which Mr. Lee demonstrated impairment), and agreed to take a breathalyzer test, which yielded a result of 0.09 blood-alcohol content at the time. The officers arrested Mr. Lee for driving under the influence, searched Mr. Lee, and found a handgun in Mr. Lee’s pants pocket. Mr. Lee’s vehicle was also towed and impounded for further investigation. Inside the vehicle, investigators found a backpack which contained a variety of bags of suspected narcotics, a digital scale, small empty clear baggies, gloves, a mask, and Mr. Lee’s driver’s license. The Department of Forensic Science tested the suspected narcotics and confirmed that the assorted bags held 27.86 grams of a fentanyl and xylazine mixture known colloquially as “tranq,” 90 fentanyl capsules, 30 tablets of methamphetamine, 0.79 grams of cocaine powder, and 40 grams of marijuana. The police charged Mr. Lee following his initial arrest with being a felon in possession of a firearm, felony second-offense carrying of a concealed firearm, misdemeanor driving while intoxicated, and misdemeanor driving with a revoked license. After confirming the narcotics Mr. Lee possessed in the vehicle, police charged Mr. Lee with additional felony counts of possessing Schedule II drugs with the intent to distribute, possessing those drugs with a firearm on his person, possessing marijuana with the intent to distribute, and possession of fentanyl with the intent to distribute. On Jan. 21, 2026, Mr. Lee entered an agreement to plead guilty to possessing Schedule II drugs with the intent to distribute, being a felon in possession of a firearm, and driving while intoxicated. The Commonwealth withdrew Mr. Lee’s additional charges, and there was no agreement as to the sentence Mr. Lee would receive. Judge Joseph C. Lindsey accepted Mr. Lee’s plea agreement and set his sentencing hearing on April 3. After hearing arguments from the Commonwealth and Mr. Lee’s defense counsel, Judge Lindsey sentenced Mr. Lee to serve five years in prison and suspended another 11 years in custody on the conditions that Mr. Lee completes the Virginia Alcohol Safety Action Program, pays a mandatory fine, has his license suspended for another 12 months, and completes three years of supervised probation and five years of uniform good behavior after his release. Judge Lindsey also found Mr. Lee in violation of his probation for a prior offense and revoked one year of his previously suspended sentence for Mr. Lee to now serve, bringing his total active sentence to six years. Mr. Lee’s sentences were consistent with his Virginia sentencing guidelines and reflected his prior criminal history. “In one criminal incident Mr. Lee did three things he should not have done that endangered the community: possess a firearm as a previously convicted felon, possessing tranq with the intent to sell, and drive drunk,” said Commonwealth’s Attorney Ramin Fatehi. “The fact that Mr. Lee was dealing tranq, which is extremely dangerous for people to use and which is resistant to overdose-reversal medicine, is particularly worrisome. Each of these offenses on its own would have exposed Mr. Lee to a conviction and jail. Together, they have earned him a trip to prison. We will continue to seek appropriate accountability for people who negatively affect public safety.” Senior Assistant Commonwealth’s Attorney Emily A. Woodley prosecuted Mr. Lee’s case, and Norfolk Police Detective James L. Luttrell led the investigation.

title: Commonwealth v. Malik Hunt Jr. - City of Norfolk (.gov)
content: NORFOLK, Va. — Malik Hunt Jr., 27, pleaded guilty on March 30 to felony carrying of a concealed weapon, being a felon in possession of a firearm, and driving on a suspended license after he was pulled over for driving recklessly and was found to have had a gun with him in the car. After 11 p.m. on Feb. 7, 2025, Norfolk Police patrol officers saw Mr. Hunt speeding and driving erratically through a residential area of Ballentine Boulevard. When the officers pulled over Mr. Hunt, Mr. Hunt admitted that he did not have a valid driver's license. The officers checked Mr. Hunt’s information in DMV records, which confirmed that Mr. Hunt had his license revoked due to a prior DUI conviction. Mr. Hunt was arrested, and the vehicle he drove was towed and impounded. When investigators subsequently searched the vehicle, they recovered a handgun that was concealed under a pile of clothing. Mr. Hunt, who has prior felony convictions (including felony second-offense carrying of a concealed weapon) and is prohibited from possessing a firearm, later admitted to having known that the firearm was there. On March 30, Mr. Hunt entered an agreement to plead guilty to felony third-offense carrying of a concealed weapon, being a felon in possession of a firearm, and misdemeanor driving with a suspended license. There was no agreement as to the sentence Mr. Hunt will receive. Judge Devon R. Paige accepted Mr. Hunt’s plea agreement, found him guilty, and set his sentencing hearing on July 17. “One of the few remaining gun-safety guardrails in American law is that people convicted of felonies should not possess guns,” said Commonwealth’s Attorney Ramin Fatehi. “Mr. Hunt should never have possessed the gun he had, and at sentencing we will argue for a sanction that is appropriate to Mr. Hunt’s actions.” Senior Assistant Commonwealth’s Attorney Stephanie G. Johnson is prosecuting Mr. Hunt’s case, and Norfolk Police Officers Seth M. Williams and John C. Ball led the investigation.

title: The New 2028 Chevy Camaro Is an American V-8-Powered Pony Car Worth Waiting For - Car and Driver
content: An American icon will return with an optional V-8 sending loads of horsepower to the rear wheels for tire-smoking shenanigans. The Chevrolet Camaro is poised to return to battle the Ford Mustang and the Dodge Charger after a brief hiatus. We expect the Camaro to continue as a two-door sports car, although it's possible that Chevy will also offer a four-door version, like Dodge does with the Charger. Regardless, the 2+2 seating arrangement should continue, with the back seats once again best suited to kids or brief trips for adults. We also think the seventh-generation Camaro will adopt a fastback design, with a sloping roofline that incorporates a hatchback and recalls the third- and fourth-gen Camaros. The original Chevy Camaro debuted for the 1967 model year as a natural rival to the hugely popular Mustang. While the nameplate went into hibernation between 2003 and 2009, the Camaro's fifth generation made a triumphant return for the 2010 model year, and the sixth-gen car followed six years later. Despite all of its accolades, including multiple 10Best awards, sales eventually began to slide, and it withered on the vine until GM ultimately axed the Camaro after the 2024 model year. This introduced a gaping hole in Chevy's performance arsenal, leaving the Corvette as the bowtie brand's most affordable sports car, albeit with a $72,000-plus starting price. The Camaro's revival will bring a more attainable performance machine to Chevrolet showrooms, and it will also demonstrate GM's commitment to internal-combustion power. See all results for new 2028 Chevrolet Camaro for sale near 22747 The 2028 Camaro will ride on an updated version of the Alpha platform, which underpinned the previous Camaro as well as the Cadillac CT4 and CT5 sedans. This reworked Alpha 2 platform will also spawn a new CT5 and a new Buick sedan. Previous reports suggested that the Camaro could use the VSS-R platform found beneath the second-gen Cadillac CT6 sold in China, but we think GM is instead overhauling the Alpha architecture that has already proven itself in the CT4-V Blackwing and the CT5-V Blackwing, both 10Best-winning sports sedans. The Alpha 2 platform is set up for front-engine, rear-wheel-drive vehicles, and we expect the new Camaro will stick to its RWD roots. Still, we think there's a chance Chevy offers all-wheel drive, since it's available on the current CT4 and CT5. Having an AWD option on the next Camaro would also likely help boost sales in areas with cold-weather climates. Plus, an all-wheel-drive Camaro would better compete with the Charger, giving it a leg up on the Mustang too. The new Camaro will pack internal-combustion engines under the hood, and we expect the base powertrain to be a turbocharged inline-four to maintain an affordable entry price and rival the four-cylinder Mustang. The Camaro will likely use a version of the turbocharged 2.7-liter four currently found in the CT4, since it's already compatible with the Alpha platform. In the CT4-V, it produces 325 horsepower and 380 pound-feet, but in the Chevy Silverado and Colorado trucks, it delivers up to 430 pound-feet. Thankfully, the Camaro will also feature an optional V-8. We expect it to borrow the new LS6 6.7-liter small-block engine that debuts in the 2027 Corvette Stingray and Grand Sport. The LS6 produces 535 horsepower and 520 pound-feet of torque in the Vette, which would be a healthy increase over the last Camaro SS. High-performance variants are surely in the pipeline, and given the Camaro's history of adopting the Corvette's powertrains, we think the Vette's optional 5.5-liter V-8 could power a future ZL1 or Z/28. In terms of transmissions, a 10-speed automatic seems likely, but we're praying that Chevy also offers a six-speed manual as it did in the last Camaro and currently does in both the CT4-V Blackwing and the CT5-V Blackwing. There's a small chance Chevy offers a V-6 engine between the four-cylinder and V-8, as it did on the last generation, but we think there's a strong likelihood that Chevy skips this middle ground to reduce complexity. We don't expect to see an electric Camaro, given the struggles of the Dodge Charger Daytona, which has seen its sales fall even lower after the arrival of its gas-powered alternative. The Camaro will go head to head with its longtime rival, the Ford Mustang, and the latest Dodge Charger. Shoppers might also consider sports cars like the 400-hp Nissan Z and the 473-hp BMW M2. The Chevy faithful will have big expectations, and GM will need to execute the Camaro perfectly if it wants to be competitive in the shrinking sports-car segment. Not offering a manual transmission or a V-8 would surely turn off many enthusiasts, and keeping the base price affordable will be crucial. Plus, a Camaro needs to look cool, and a botched design could prove to be an immediate turnoff for muscle-car shoppers. The Camaro will be built alongside its Cadillac and Buick platform-mates at the Lansing Grand River Assembly plant in Michigan, with production set to begin in late 2027. It will likely go on sale for the 2028 model year. We think Chevy will try to keep the base price fairly attainable, to help distinguish the Camaro from Chevy's other sports car, the Corvette. We expect that a four-cylinder Camaro will start around $40,000, while a V-8 model will likely start over $50,000. ➡️ Skip the lot. Let Car and Driver help you find your next car. Caleb Miller began blogging about cars at 13 years old, and he realized his dream of writing for a car magazine after graduating from Carnegie Mellon University and joining the Car and Driver team. He loves quirky and obscure autos, aiming to one day own something bizarre like a Nissan S-Cargo, and is an avid motorsports fan. We may earn a commission for purchases made through our links.
